artifacts.content:
Hi Tom,

I’m hiding in the kitchen while the pasta water boils, so this will be a little rambly.

I’ve been at Lakeside more than usual this month. Between checking on Aunt Pat upstairs and dealing with the 1F tenants, it feels like I practically live at 1845 again. Michael on the first floor is still on electronic home monitoring after that 2023 DUI, so he’s home in the unit most weekdays from about 8:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., juggling his software job for the downtown Chicago office and those court‑ordered alcohol counseling sessions at AMITA Health St. Francis in Evanston. I try to schedule any noisy work around his counseling days so he’s not totally overwhelmed.

Meanwhile, Pat has really turned the smaller bedroom in the second‑floor unit into a little home health recovery space since her mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital back in October 2022. Her visiting nurse from NorthShore Home Health, Karen L., comes by three mornings a week for about an hour to check her blood pressure, meds, and do light PT. It’s actually kind of sweet watching them go through their routine while I’m puttering in the kitchen.

All of that makes me think about when you were camped out in the unfinished basement on that air mattress near the boiler last January–March, when your panic attacks got so bad you took FMLA from AzureBeam and didn’t feel safe being alone in your Seattle apartment. I’m still glad you were here, even if it was just concrete floors and storage boxes.

How are you really doing now? Are the attacks any better, or just different?

Love,
Laura

Personal note – Lakeside caregiving + kids

Date: 2025-03-03

I need one place to brain‑dump how all these pieces fit together.

1845 Lakeside is still “just” a two‑flat, but it feels like a whole ecosystem. Unit 1: young family, 12‑month lease that started March 1, 2024. Dad (Michael R.) is on electronic home monitoring after the 2023 DUI, so he’s basically in the first‑floor unit 8–6 on weekdays, bouncing between his work‑from‑home software engineering job for the downtown Chicago office and the court‑ordered alcohol counseling sessions at AMITA Health St. Francis Hospital. Their little boy has weekly occupational therapy at NorthShore Evanston Hospital on Tuesdays at 4:00 p.m. and almost always comes back to play in the backyard afterward – need to remember not to schedule gutter work then.

Unit 2: Aunt Pat has been there since June 2013. Since her mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital in October 2022, one bedroom doubles as a home health recovery space. Her visiting nurse from NorthShore Home Health, Karen L., comes three mornings a week for ~1 hour (BP, meds, light PT). Pat also uses the rear deck for low‑impact exercises from her Athletico Evanston PT plan, when the weather cooperates.

Basement: unfinished, storage + mechanicals + laundry only. Reminder: January–March 2023, Tom slept on an air mattress near the boiler for about six weeks while on FMLA from AzureBeam because his panic attacks were so bad he didn’t feel safe alone in Seattle. No rent, no separate unit, no STR.

Garage: standard parking – first‑floor tenants + me when I’m there.

Overlay all of that with Sofia and Diego’s school schedules, and it’s a lot of moving parts.

Hi Mark,

I owe you about ten phone calls, so here’s a long email instead.

Pat is hanging in there at Lakeside. Hard to believe she’s been in that second‑floor unit since June 2013. After her mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital in October 2022, I turned the smaller bedroom into a makeshift recovery space. Her visiting nurse from NorthShore Home Health, Karen L., still comes by three mornings a week for about an hour – checks her blood pressure, manages the post‑stroke meds, runs her through those simple physical therapy exercises. On good days, they migrate out to the rear deck so Pat can do the low‑impact stuff her Athletico Evanston therapist recommended.

Downstairs, the first‑floor tenants are doing fine. Their lease started March 1, 2024. The dad, Michael, is that software engineer I told you about – he’s on electronic home monitoring after his 2023 DUI, so he’s home most weekdays 8–6, either logged into his company’s downtown Chicago office or heading over to AMITA Health St. Francis for those court‑ordered alcohol counseling sessions. Their little boy has OT at NorthShore on Tuesdays at 4:00 p.m., then comes back and tears around the backyard. It’s actually kind of nice background noise when I’m there.

Every time I’m in the basement switching laundry, I think about when Tom was sleeping on that air mattress near the boiler January–March 2023, during his FMLA leave from AzureBeam. Concrete floor, exposed pipes, and somehow that was the only place he felt safe being alone.

Anyway, that’s the Lakeside update. How are things on your end? Any plans to get out here this summer so you can sit on Pat’s three‑season porch and let her quiz you on 1970s mystery novels?

Love,
Laura

Event: Overnight at Lakeside – Sofia support + property check

Date: 2024-05-16, 5:30 p.m. – 2024-05-17, 8:00 a.m.
Location: 1845 Lakeside Drive (park in detached two‑car garage)

Notes:

– Drive over after picking Sofia up from Nichols orientation. She’s still really anxious about starting at Nichols Middle School in the fall after the bullying incident at Oakton Elementary during 3rd grade that got bad enough we ended up in that meeting with the school social worker, Ms. Ramirez. Plan: debrief with her over pizza, let her vent, maybe walk her through the halls again in her head so it feels less scary.

– Park in my usual spot in the detached garage behind 1845. Garage is still strictly residential parking: one space for the first‑floor tenants, one for me when I stay overnight.

– While there, quick walkthrough:
   • Check on Aunt Pat in Unit 2, make sure the home health “recovery bedroom” is set up for Karen’s NorthShore Home Health visits (BP cuff, meds list on nightstand).
   • Confirm with Pat that she’s still doing her low‑impact PT exercises from Athletico Evanston on the rear deck when the weather’s decent.
   • Say a quick hello to 1F tenants; remember Michael is on electronic home monitoring after the 2023 DUI and usually home 8–6, plus juggling his counseling at AMITA Health St. Francis.

– Morning: drive Sofia to school, then swing by Orrington office.

Anna: How’s Lakeside today?

Laura: Busy but okay. Michael downstairs is on another long WFH day – ankle monitor + all that after the 2023 DUI means he’s basically in the 1F unit 8–6, except when he heads to AMITA St. Francis for those counseling sessions. I tried to sneak in between his Zooms to look at the boiler.

Anna: Poor guy. Did you see Pat?

Laura: Yep. Went up to 2F around 10. Karen from NorthShore Home Health was just finishing – she still comes three mornings a week, checks Pat’s BP, meds, and runs through the post‑stroke exercises. Hard to believe it’s been since that mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital in Oct 2022.

Anna: Is Pat still doing the deck exercises?

Laura: When it’s not freezing. She shuffles out to the rear deck and does the low‑impact PT her Athletico Evanston therapist gave her. Meanwhile the 1F tenants’ little boy came back from his OT at NorthShore (Tues 4 pm like clockwork) and was tearing around the backyard. Kinda sweet – two very different “rehab programs” happening in the same little yard.

Anna: That house has seen everything.

Laura: No kidding. Walking through the basement I flashed back to Jan–March 2023 when Tom was sleeping on that air mattress near the boiler during his FMLA from AzureBeam. Concrete floor, panic attacks, space heater, and somehow he said it felt safer than his Seattle apartment.

Anna: We’ve collected a lot of strays in that building, huh? ;)

Laura: Yep. I’ll be home by 6 – can you start pasta?

Hi Ms. Ramirez,

I wanted to touch base now that we’re edging closer to Sofia’s full transition to Nichols.

She’s still carrying a lot from what happened at Oakton Elementary in 3rd grade. That bullying incident that ended up in your office – I can still picture her sitting there with you, twisting her sleeves while we talked through what the other girls had been saying. I’m grateful you took it seriously and helped us put a plan in place, but it definitely left a mark.

To give her a little extra stability, I’ve been spending more time at my duplex at 1845 Lakeside this spring and early summer, parking in the detached two‑car garage and staying overnight a couple nights a week. It’s only a few minutes’ drive from both Oakton and Nichols, so on the days when her anxiety spikes, I can swing by quickly for drop‑off or pick‑up.

Those nights at Lakeside are a bit of a juggling act because the building is its own little universe. My aunt Pat lives in the second‑floor unit; since her mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital in October 2022, one of her bedrooms is basically a home health recovery space. Her nurse from NorthShore Home Health, Karen, stops by three mornings a week to check blood pressure, meds, and do some PT. Downstairs, the first‑floor tenants’ young son has OT at NorthShore on Tuesdays at 4:00 p.m. and burns off energy in the backyard afterward while his dad, Michael, works from home under electronic monitoring after a DUI.

All that to say: there’s a lot going on, but Sofia is the priority. If you have recommendations for how we talk about Nichols this summer so it feels like a fresh start, I’m all ears.

Best,
Laura Kensington

metadata.subject: Sofia’s transition to Nichols – summer support

artifacts.content:
Working session – long‑term plan for 1845 Lakeside

Date: 2025-06-22
Location: Home office, 742 Evergreen Terrace, Suite 300, Evanston, IL 60201

Brainstorming how to keep 1845 sustainable without losing the human side.

1. Current occupants
– Unit 1 (1F): Young family, 12‑month lease started 3/1/24. Dad, Michael R., is a software engineer tied to his company’s downtown Chicago office but on electronic home monitoring after a 2023 DUI. He’s in the unit most weekdays 8:00 a.m.–6:00 p.m., except when he’s at AMITA Health St. Francis Hospital in Evanston for court‑ordered alcohol counseling. Their son’s weekly OT at NorthShore Evanston Hospital (Tuesdays at 4:00 p.m.) means predictable backyard playtime afterward.

– Unit 2 (2F): Aunt Patricia “Pat” Hart, there since June 2013. Mild stroke at NorthShore Evanston Hospital in Oct 2022. One bedroom now functions as a home health recovery room. Karen L. from NorthShore Home Health visits three mornings/week (~1 hr) for BP, meds, light PT. Pat uses the rear deck for low‑impact exercises per Athletico Evanston’s plan.

– Basement: Unfinished, used for storage/mechanicals/laundry only. Reminder to myself: January–March 2023, Tom slept on an air mattress near the boiler for ~6 weeks during his FMLA leave from AzureBeam because his panic attacks made him afraid to be alone in his Seattle apartment. No rent, no separate dwelling unit, no advertising.

– Garage: Detached two‑car, alley access. One space for 1F tenants, one for me. I used it heavily during May–June 2024 when I stayed over to support Sofia through her Nichols Middle School anxiety after the Oakton bullying incident and the meeting with Ms. Ramirez.

2. To‑dos
– Explore accessibility upgrades for Pat.
– Confirm any zoning implications if Pat’s care needs increase.
– Keep rents stable for 1F as long as finances allow.
